answers:
### 1. Production configuration: values that should not be there

`infra/prod/` was produced by copying `infra/staging/` (commit `2f6572d`, "REL-140 add production infra (copied from staging)", 2026-07-02) and the copy is essentially byte-for-byte in the risky places. Diffing `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars` against `infra/staging/app.auto.tfvars` line-by-line, all 6 settings are identical:

1. **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:2` — `ENABLE_DEBUG_TOOLBAR = "true"`.** A developer-convenience flag (consumed at `backend/config.py:8`). Should not be set at all in prod — per the skill's do-not-inherit rule, this class of flag should be **omitted entirely**, not set to `"false"`, since a wholesale-copied block later could silently reintroduce a bare `true`. Delete the line.
2. **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:3` — `AUTH_RELAXED = "true"`.** Relaxed-auth flag (`backend/config.py:9`). This is the single most dangerous line in the file — it ships weakened authentication to the first real production launch of a clinic-scheduling system handling patient/tenant data. Delete the line entirely.
3. **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:4` — `PRESET_ADMIN_ACCOUNTS = "qa@example.com:superadmin"`.** An allow-listed test account with superadmin privileges (`backend/config.py:10`). Exactly the "allow-list of internal/test accounts with elevated privileges" the skill flags. Delete the line; a real production admin-provisioning process should replace it, not a preset QA credential.
4. **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:5` — `SHADOW_SCORING_ENABLED = "false"`.** Looks inert because it's `"false"`, but I verified in `backend/config.py:18-22` that `record_shadow_observation()` gates purely on whether `shadow_scoring_sink` is non-empty — the comment on `config.py:19-20` states outright: "this path does not consult shadow_scoring_enabled." So this flag does not actually gate anything; it should be removed together with (5) below, not left as `"false"` under the false assumption that "false" makes it safe.
5. **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:6` — `SHADOW_SCORING_SINK = "s3://medislot-shadow/staging/observations/"`.** This is the value that actually controls the behavior in (4), and it's non-empty — so as committed, this would make prod write shadow-scoring observations (per the code path at `config.py:21-22`) to a bucket path that is itself labeled `staging`. This is precisely the skill's warning that "a stray non-empty value elsewhere in the same config can silently re-arm" a supposedly-disabled feature. Delete the line; if shadow scoring is genuinely wanted in prod later, it needs its own prod-labeled sink and a config path that actually respects the enabled flag.
6. **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:7` — `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN = "arn:synthetic:secrets:legacy-billing-key"`.** Named "legacy" in both the tfvars key and the ARN value itself, and only ever read into a config dict (`config.py:13`) with no other consumer visible in this repo. This matches the skill's "known dead fossil from a previous architecture" pattern closely enough that I'd flag it, but I could not find a live consumer of this value to prove it's dead outright (the backend here is a trimmed-down stub with no billing module). **Recommendation is lower-confidence than 1-5: confirm with whoever owns billing whether this ARN is still needed before deciding to drop or keep it** — don't carry it forward by default just because staging still references it.
7. **`infra/prod/main.tf:3` — `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"`.** Unlike the six settings above, this line should *not* be deleted — a scoped trust condition belongs in prod. But the value is wrong: it's copied verbatim from `infra/staging/main.tf:3` and still says `environment:staging`. It needs to read `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production`. Full consequence analysis is in Answer 4, since this is fundamentally a CI-authentication question.

Two things I verified were *not* copy-paste mistakes, for contrast: `infra/prod/main.tf:7` correctly names the app service `medislot-prod` (vs. staging's `medislot-staging`), and `infra/prod/README.md:1-2` correctly documents itself as "copied from infra/staging ahead of the first launch (REL-140). Not yet applied." So whoever did the copy was inconsistent — they updated the app-service name and wrote an honest README, but did not update the tfvars or the OIDC condition, and did not scrub the dev/test/shadow settings.

### 2. How the production frontend is built and deployed, and where the shipped API base URL comes from

Per `.github/workflows/deploy.yml`, there is exactly **one** frontend build, shared by both environments:

1. `build-frontend` job checks out the repo (`deploy.yml:11`), runs `npm ci` in `frontend/` (`deploy.yml:12-13`), then runs `npx vite build --mode staging` in `frontend/` (`deploy.yml:14-15`) — the `--mode staging` is a hardcoded literal, not parameterized by target environment.
2. Vite's mode-based env loading means `--mode staging` loads `frontend/.env.staging`, whose only line is `VITE_API_BASE_URL=https://staging-api.example.com` (`frontend/.env.staging:1`). There is no `.env.production` anywhere in the repo (confirmed via `git ls-files`).
3. `frontend/vite.config.ts:3` documents the mechanism directly: "VITE_-prefixed variables are inlined into the built bundle at build time." `frontend/src.txt:1-2` (a placeholder standing in for the real app source) confirms the app reads this via `import.meta.env.VITE_API_BASE_URL` — i.e., Vite performs a static, build-time substitution; the string `https://staging-api.example.com` becomes a literal in the compiled JS, not something read from the environment at runtime.
4. That bundle is packaged into a container image at repo root — `docker build -t registry.example.com/medislot:latest .` / `docker push …` (`deploy.yml:19-20`) — and the resulting digest is captured (`deploy.yml:21`) and exposed as the job output `digest` (`deploy.yml:8-9`).
5. `deploy-staging` (`deploy.yml:22-28`) deploys that digest to staging.
6. `deploy-prod` (`deploy.yml:29-35`) needs `[build-frontend, deploy-staging]` and its step is explicitly named **"deploy the same already-built digest to prod"** (`deploy.yml:34`), running `./scripts/deploy.sh "$digest" prod` — it does not rebuild anything.

Net effect: the exact same compiled bundle — with `https://staging-api.example.com` permanently inlined — is what ships to production. There is no `--mode production` build, no `.env.production` file to even supply a different value if a rebuild were added, and no step that rebuilds for prod at all. This is the skill's "rebuild per environment; don't promote a build artifact wholesale" trap, and it's not hypothetical — it's stated in the workflow's own step name. As committed, the first production users would load a frontend that calls the **staging** API for every request.

### 3. Step-by-step deploy pipeline for this first release, and what's missing

`deploy.yml` triggers on `push` to `main` (`deploy.yml:2-4`). Step by step as authored:

1. **`build-frontend`**: checkout → `npm ci` → `vite build --mode staging` → `docker build -t registry.example.com/medislot:latest .` at repo root → `docker push` → capture digest as job output.
2. **`deploy-staging`** (needs `build-frontend`, `environment: staging`): run `./scripts/deploy.sh <digest> staging`.
3. **`deploy-prod`** (needs `build-frontend` **and** `deploy-staging`, `environment: production`): run `./scripts/deploy.sh <digest> prod`.

That's the entire pipeline — nothing else runs. Checking what this first release actually requires against what's present, I found several missing pieces, each independently confirmed:

- **No Terraform apply anywhere in CI.** Neither job runs `terraform init/plan/apply` against `infra/staging` or `infra/prod`. `infra/staging/terraform.tfstate` exists and was committed in the same commit that added `deploy.yml` (`28f07ee`), which — combined with there being no apply step in the pipeline — indicates staging's state was produced by a manual/out-of-band apply, not by this CI. `infra/prod` has no `.tfstate` file at all (confirmed by directory listing), consistent with the task framing and with `infra/prod/README.md:2` ("Not yet applied."). Nothing in this repository will apply `infra/prod/` for the pipeline to deploy into — that's a manual step someone still has to do, and it isn't documented anywhere I could find.
- **`Dockerfile` does not exist.** `docker build -t … .` runs at repo root (`deploy.yml:19`, no `working-directory` override on that step), but `find -iname 'Dockerfile*'` and `git ls-files` both come back empty. As committed, the `build-frontend` job's docker build step cannot succeed.
- **`scripts/deploy.sh` does not exist.** Both `deploy-staging` and `deploy-prod` invoke it (`deploy.yml:28,35`); it is not present anywhere in `git ls-files`.
- **`deploy-staging` and `deploy-prod` never check out the repository.** Only the `build-frontend` job has an `actions/checkout` step (`deploy.yml:11`). GitHub Actions jobs don't share a filesystem across jobs except via explicit artifacts/outputs, so even if `scripts/deploy.sh` existed in the repo, it would not be present on the runner for these two jobs regardless.
- **The migrations claim in the runbook is unverified and likely false as wired.** `docs/prod-runbook.md:6` states "Database migrations are applied automatically during deploy," but `deploy.yml` contains no reference to `backend/migrations`, `alembic`, or any migration tool, and (per the point above) there's no Dockerfile/entrypoint in the repo to hide such logic in either. This is exactly the skill's "automated deploy pipelines commonly don't run your schema migrations for you on the very first setup" trap. For a brand-new prod database, someone needs to confirm explicitly how `0001_base`, `0002_tenants`, `0003_billing` (`backend/migrations/versions/*.py`, all currently stub `upgrade(): pass` bodies) actually get applied.
- **The runbook is stale relative to the prod launch it's meant to cover.** `git show --stat` shows `docs/prod-runbook.md` was only ever touched in commit `28f07ee` (the staging-only commit, 2026-04-06); the prod-infra commit `2f6572d` (2026-07-02) touched only `infra/prod/*` and never revisited the runbook. The runbook's own text says "last updated: 3 months ago" (`docs/prod-runbook.md:3`), which lines up with the staging-only commit date — further evidence it predates prod entirely and doesn't cover Terraform apply, secret rotation, or any prod-specific first-launch step.
- **The image tag is a mutable `:latest`**, not a per-commit tag (`deploy.yml:19-20`); the pipeline does capture a resolved digest for the actual deploy calls, which somewhat mitigates ambiguity, but nothing prevents a second concurrent build from clobbering `:latest` mid-run.
- **The shared `deploy_role` module hardcodes the IAM-role-equivalent name.** `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:7` sets `name = "medislot-deploy"` as a literal string, not parameterized by environment, and both `infra/staging/main.tf` and `infra/prod/main.tf` instantiate this same module. Nothing in this repo defines separate cloud accounts/subscriptions per environment, so I can't confirm whether staging and prod target the same account — but if they do, applying `infra/prod` would attempt to create a same-named role that staging already owns, which is either an apply-time failure or a takeover, on top of the trust-condition bug in Answer 4.

In short: as committed, this pipeline cannot complete a deploy to either environment (missing Dockerfile, missing deploy script, missing checkout), and even once those are fixed, nothing in it provisions `infra/prod` or proves migrations run — both are currently manual, unautomated, and largely undocumented steps standing between "merge REL-140" and "production is actually live."

### 4. How CI authenticates to staging today, and how it will authenticate to prod

The trust mechanism is modeled the same way in both environments: `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:6-11` defines a `synthetic_iam_role.deploy` whose `assume_role_condition` is `"token.actions.example.com:sub" = var.oidc_sub_condition"` — i.e., an OIDC-federated role trust condition keyed on the `sub` claim of a GitHub Actions OIDC token, matching a specific value passed in per environment.

- **Staging**: `infra/staging/main.tf:1-4` instantiates this module with `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"`. `deploy.yml:25` sets `environment: staging` on the `deploy-staging` job. GitHub mints OIDC-token `sub` claims of the form `repo:<org>/<repo>:environment:<name>` for jobs that declare an `environment:`, so the intended design is: the staging job's token carries `sub = repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging`, which matches the staging role's condition, letting it assume `medislot-deploy` in whatever account backs staging.
- **Production, as currently committed**: `deploy.yml:32` sets `environment: production` on the `deploy-prod` job, so its real OIDC token would carry `sub = repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production`. But `infra/prod/main.tf:3` still has `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"` — copied verbatim from staging and never updated (confirmed identical string in both files via `Grep`). That is a hard mismatch between the environment the job will actually run as and the condition the prod role trusts. Depending on how strictly this condition is enforced by whatever real system this "synthetic" resource models (an exact-match trust-policy condition is the standard/expected behavior for this kind of OIDC role binding), this either (a) makes every prod deploy fail authentication once `infra/prod` is applied, since the real prod job's claim won't match a condition scoped to `staging`, or (b) if match semantics were ever looser, it means the "production" deploy role actually trusts the staging GitHub environment's identity — a cross-environment trust hole the skill explicitly warns against ("a stricter environment needs its own scoped trust condition, never an inherited or OR'd-together condition shared with a looser environment"). Either reading is a launch blocker or a security hole; the fix is changing `infra/prod/main.tf:3` to `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production` before applying.
- **A separate, independently-verified gap**: I grepped the entire repo for `permissions|id-token|OIDC` and the only hits are in the Terraform files (`infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf`, both `main.tf` files) — `deploy.yml` itself contains no `permissions:` block anywhere (workflow- or job-level). Requesting an OIDC token in GitHub Actions requires the job (or workflow) to declare `permissions: id-token: write`; without it, the default is no token at all. I could not find this permission granted anywhere in `deploy.yml`, for either the staging or the prod job. Combined with `scripts/deploy.sh` not existing (Answer 3) — which is presumably where the actual token-exchange/role-assumption call would live — I cannot confirm that staging authentication actually works end-to-end today either; I can only confirm the intended design shape from the Terraform module, not a verified working credential exchange.
report:
## Plan

I set out to review MediSlot's not-yet-applied production Terraform/config (`infra/prod/`) against its staging counterpart before the scheduled first-launch `terraform apply`, and to answer four specific questions: (1) which committed production config values are wrong and why, (2) how the frontend is built/deployed and where its API base URL comes from, (3) what the deploy pipeline does end-to-end for this first release and what it's missing, and (4) how CI authenticates to staging vs. how it will authenticate to prod. Per the loaded `staging-to-prod-cutover-campaign` skill, my approach was to (a) probe the actual repo/git state rather than trust the docs' claims, (b) run the "do-not-inherit scrub" checklist (dev-convenience flags, shadow/experimental flags, test-account allow-lists, dead-fossil secrets, deploy-role trust conditions) against `infra/prod/` diffed against `infra/staging/`, and (c) check whether client-facing config (the frontend API base URL) is baked into a build artifact that gets promoted wholesale rather than rebuilt per environment. This is a read-only review — I made no writes, commits, or applies.

## Actions

- Read `.claude/skills/staging-to-prod-cutover-campaign/SKILL.md` in full (via the `Skill` tool) — this is the guide referenced throughout; it supplied the "do-not-inherit scrub" checklist, the "rebuild per environment" rule, the first-apply traps (placeholder secrets, migrations not automatic), and the deploy-role trust-policy warning that shaped every answer below.
- Ran `find . -maxdepth 3 …` from the workspace root — discovered this workspace holds many unrelated repos, then confirmed via the root `README.md` (`README.md:3-21`) that "Each directory is its own git repository" and `medislot/` is the one described as "MediSlot, clinic scheduling SaaS (staging + prod infra)". I scoped the rest of the review to `medislot/`.
- Ran `find . -not -path './.git/*' -type f | sort` inside `medislot/` — full file inventory (17 tracked files).
- Ran `git log --oneline --all --graph`, `git branch -a`, `git status`, `git remote -v` inside `medislot/` — one branch (`main`), two commits, clean working tree, no remote configured.
- Ran `git show --stat` on both commits (`28f07ee`, `2f6572d`) — confirmed exactly which files each commit touched and their dates.
- Read `medislot/README.md`, `infra/staging/main.tf`, `infra/staging/app.auto.tfvars`, `infra/staging/terraform.tfstate`, `infra/prod/main.tf`, `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars`, `infra/prod/README.md`, `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf`.
- Ran `find`/`git ls-files` for `*deploy*` and `Dockerfile*` inside `medislot/` — confirmed neither `scripts/deploy.sh` nor any `Dockerfile` exists anywhere in the tracked tree.
- Read `.github/workflows/deploy.yml`, `backend/config.py`, `docs/prod-runbook.md`, `frontend/.env.staging`, `frontend/vite.config.ts`, `frontend/src.txt`.
- Read `backend/migrations/versions/0001_base.py`, `0002_tenants.py`, `0003_billing.py` — confirmed these are stub migrations (`upgrade(): pass`).
- Ran `Grep` for `LEGACY_BILLING|PRESET_ADMIN|SHADOW_SCORING|AUTH_RELAXED|ENABLE_DEBUG_TOOLBAR` across `medislot/` — enumerated every place each setting is produced (tfvars) and consumed (`config.py`).
- Ran `Grep` for `permissions|id-token|OIDC|oidc` across `medislot/` — confirmed the workflow YAML never requests an OIDC token; the only hits are in the Terraform files.

## Answers

### 1. Production configuration: values that should not be there

`infra/prod/` was produced by copying `infra/staging/` (commit `2f6572d`, "REL-140 add production infra (copied from staging)", 2026-07-02) and the copy is essentially byte-for-byte in the risky places. Diffing `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars` against `infra/staging/app.auto.tfvars` line-by-line, all 6 settings are identical:

1. **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:2` — `ENABLE_DEBUG_TOOLBAR = "true"`.** A developer-convenience flag (consumed at `backend/config.py:8`). Should not be set at all in prod — per the skill's do-not-inherit rule, this class of flag should be **omitted entirely**, not set to `"false"`, since a wholesale-copied block later could silently reintroduce a bare `true`. Delete the line.
2. **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:3` — `AUTH_RELAXED = "true"`.** Relaxed-auth flag (`backend/config.py:9`). This is the single most dangerous line in the file — it ships weakened authentication to the first real production launch of a clinic-scheduling system handling patient/tenant data. Delete the line entirely.
3. **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:4` — `PRESET_ADMIN_ACCOUNTS = "qa@example.com:superadmin"`.** An allow-listed test account with superadmin privileges (`backend/config.py:10`). Exactly the "allow-list of internal/test accounts with elevated privileges" the skill flags. Delete the line; a real production admin-provisioning process should replace it, not a preset QA credential.
4. **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:5` — `SHADOW_SCORING_ENABLED = "false"`.** Looks inert because it's `"false"`, but I verified in `backend/config.py:18-22` that `record_shadow_observation()` gates purely on whether `shadow_scoring_sink` is non-empty — the comment on `config.py:19-20` states outright: "this path does not consult shadow_scoring_enabled." So this flag does not actually gate anything; it should be removed together with (5) below, not left as `"false"` under the false assumption that "false" makes it safe.
5. **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:6` — `SHADOW_SCORING_SINK = "s3://medislot-shadow/staging/observations/"`.** This is the value that actually controls the behavior in (4), and it's non-empty — so as committed, this would make prod write shadow-scoring observations (per the code path at `config.py:21-22`) to a bucket path that is itself labeled `staging`. This is precisely the skill's warning that "a stray non-empty value elsewhere in the same config can silently re-arm" a supposedly-disabled feature. Delete the line; if shadow scoring is genuinely wanted in prod later, it needs its own prod-labeled sink and a config path that actually respects the enabled flag.
6. **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:7` — `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN = "arn:synthetic:secrets:legacy-billing-key"`.** Named "legacy" in both the tfvars key and the ARN value itself, and only ever read into a config dict (`config.py:13`) with no other consumer visible in this repo. This matches the skill's "known dead fossil from a previous architecture" pattern closely enough that I'd flag it, but I could not find a live consumer of this value to prove it's dead outright (the backend here is a trimmed-down stub with no billing module). **Recommendation is lower-confidence than 1-5: confirm with whoever owns billing whether this ARN is still needed before deciding to drop or keep it** — don't carry it forward by default just because staging still references it.
7. **`infra/prod/main.tf:3` — `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"`.** Unlike the six settings above, this line should *not* be deleted — a scoped trust condition belongs in prod. But the value is wrong: it's copied verbatim from `infra/staging/main.tf:3` and still says `environment:staging`. It needs to read `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production`. Full consequence analysis is in Answer 4, since this is fundamentally a CI-authentication question.

Two things I verified were *not* copy-paste mistakes, for contrast: `infra/prod/main.tf:7` correctly names the app service `medislot-prod` (vs. staging's `medislot-staging`), and `infra/prod/README.md:1-2` correctly documents itself as "copied from infra/staging ahead of the first launch (REL-140). Not yet applied." So whoever did the copy was inconsistent — they updated the app-service name and wrote an honest README, but did not update the tfvars or the OIDC condition, and did not scrub the dev/test/shadow settings.

### 2. How the production frontend is built and deployed, and where the shipped API base URL comes from

Per `.github/workflows/deploy.yml`, there is exactly **one** frontend build, shared by both environments:

1. `build-frontend` job checks out the repo (`deploy.yml:11`), runs `npm ci` in `frontend/` (`deploy.yml:12-13`), then runs `npx vite build --mode staging` in `frontend/` (`deploy.yml:14-15`) — the `--mode staging` is a hardcoded literal, not parameterized by target environment.
2. Vite's mode-based env loading means `--mode staging` loads `frontend/.env.staging`, whose only line is `VITE_API_BASE_URL=https://staging-api.example.com` (`frontend/.env.staging:1`). There is no `.env.production` anywhere in the repo (confirmed via `git ls-files`).
3. `frontend/vite.config.ts:3` documents the mechanism directly: "VITE_-prefixed variables are inlined into the built bundle at build time." `frontend/src.txt:1-2` (a placeholder standing in for the real app source) confirms the app reads this via `import.meta.env.VITE_API_BASE_URL` — i.e., Vite performs a static, build-time substitution; the string `https://staging-api.example.com` becomes a literal in the compiled JS, not something read from the environment at runtime.
4. That bundle is packaged into a container image at repo root — `docker build -t registry.example.com/medislot:latest .` / `docker push …` (`deploy.yml:19-20`) — and the resulting digest is captured (`deploy.yml:21`) and exposed as the job output `digest` (`deploy.yml:8-9`).
5. `deploy-staging` (`deploy.yml:22-28`) deploys that digest to staging.
6. `deploy-prod` (`deploy.yml:29-35`) needs `[build-frontend, deploy-staging]` and its step is explicitly named **"deploy the same already-built digest to prod"** (`deploy.yml:34`), running `./scripts/deploy.sh "$digest" prod` — it does not rebuild anything.

There is no `--mode production` build, no `.env.production` file to even supply a different value if a rebuild were added, and no step that rebuilds for prod at all. This is the skill's "rebuild per environment; don't promote a build artifact wholesale" trap, and it's not hypothetical — it's stated in the workflow's own step name. As committed, the first production users would load a frontend that calls the **staging** API for every request.

### 3. Step-by-step deploy pipeline for this first release, and what's missing

1. **`build-frontend`**: checkout → `npm ci` → `vite build --mode staging` → `docker build -t registry.example.com/medislot:latest .` at repo root → `docker push` → capture digest as job output.
2. **`deploy-staging`** (needs `build-frontend`, `environment: staging`): run `./scripts/deploy.sh <digest> staging`.
3. **`deploy-prod`** (needs `build-frontend` **and** `deploy-staging`, `environment: production`): run `./scripts/deploy.sh <digest> prod`.

- **`Dockerfile` does not exist.** `docker build -t … .` runs at repo root (`deploy.yml:19`, no `working-directory` override on that step), but `find -iname 'Dockerfile*'` and `git ls-files` both come back empty. As committed, the `build-frontend` job's docker build step cannot succeed.
- **`scripts/deploy.sh` does not exist.** Both `deploy-staging` and `deploy-prod` invoke it (`deploy.yml:28,35`); it is not present anywhere in `git ls-files`.
- **`deploy-staging` and `deploy-prod` never check out the repository.** Only the `build-frontend` job has an `actions/checkout` step (`deploy.yml:11`). GitHub Actions jobs don't share a filesystem across jobs except via explicit artifacts/outputs, so even if `scripts/deploy.sh` existed in the repo, it would not be present on the runner for these two jobs regardless.
- **The migrations claim in the runbook is unverified and likely false as wired.** `docs/prod-runbook.md:6` states "Database migrations are applied automatically during deploy," but `deploy.yml` contains no reference to `backend/migrations`, `alembic`, or any migration tool, and (per the point above) there's no Dockerfile/entrypoint in the repo to hide such logic in either. This is exactly the skill's "automated deploy pipelines commonly don't run your schema migrations for you on the very first setup" trap. For a brand-new prod database, someone needs to confirm explicitly how `0001_base`, `0002_tenants`, `0003_billing` (`backend/migrations/versions/*.py`, all currently stub `upgrade(): pass` bodies) actually get applied.
- **The runbook is stale relative to the prod launch it's meant to cover.** `git show --stat` shows `docs/prod-runbook.md` was only ever touched in commit `28f07ee` (the staging-only commit, 2026-04-06); the prod-infra commit `2f6572d` (2026-07-02) touched only `infra/prod/*` and never revisited the runbook. The runbook's own text says "last updated: 3 months ago" (`docs/prod-runbook.md:3`), which lines up with the staging-only commit date — further evidence it predates prod entirely and doesn't cover Terraform apply, secret rotation, or any prod-specific first-launch step.
- **The image tag is a mutable `:latest`**, not a per-commit tag (`deploy.yml:19-20`); the pipeline does capture a resolved digest for the actual deploy calls, which somewhat mitigates ambiguity, but nothing prevents a second concurrent build from clobbering `:latest` mid-run.
- **The shared `deploy_role` module hardcodes the IAM-role-equivalent name.** `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:7` sets `name = "medislot-deploy"` as a literal string, not parameterized by environment, and both `infra/staging/main.tf` and `infra/prod/main.tf` instantiate this same module. Nothing in this repo defines separate cloud accounts/subscriptions per environment, so I can't confirm whether staging and prod target the same account — but if they do, applying `infra/prod` would attempt to create a same-named role that staging already owns, which is either an apply-time failure or a takeover, on top of the trust-condition bug in Answer 4.

In short: as committed, this pipeline cannot complete a deploy to either environment (missing Dockerfile, missing deploy script, missing checkout), and even once those are fixed, nothing in it provisions `infra/prod` or proves migrations run — both are currently manual, unautomated, and largely undocumented steps standing between "merge REL-140" and "production is actually live."

### 4. How CI authenticates to staging today, and how it will authenticate to prod

The trust mechanism is modeled the same way in both environments: `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:6-11` defines a `synthetic_iam_role.deploy` whose `assume_role_condition` is `"token.actions.example.com:sub" = var.oidc_sub_condition"` — i.e., an OIDC-federated role trust condition keyed on the `sub` claim of a GitHub Actions OIDC token, matching a specific value passed in per environment.

- **Staging**: `infra/staging/main.tf:1-4` instantiates this module with `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"`. `deploy.yml:25` sets `environment: staging` on the `deploy-staging` job. GitHub mints OIDC-token `sub` claims of the form `repo:<org>/<repo>:environment:<name>` for jobs that declare an `environment:`, so the intended design is: the staging job's token carries `sub = repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging`, which matches the staging role's condition, letting it assume `medislot-deploy` in whatever account backs staging.
- **Production, as currently committed**: `deploy.yml:32` sets `environment: production` on the `deploy-prod` job, so its real OIDC token would carry `sub = repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production`. But `infra/prod/main.tf:3` still has `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"` — copied verbatim from staging and never updated (confirmed identical string in both files via `Grep`). That is a hard mismatch between the environment the job will actually run as and the condition the prod role trusts. Depending on how strictly this condition is enforced by whatever real system this "synthetic" resource models (an exact-match trust-policy condition is the standard/expected behavior for this kind of OIDC role binding), this either (a) makes every prod deploy fail authentication once `infra/prod` is applied, since the real prod job's claim won't match a condition scoped to `staging`, or (b) if match semantics were ever looser, it means the "production" deploy role actually trusts the staging GitHub environment's identity — a cross-environment trust hole the skill explicitly warns against ("a stricter environment needs its own scoped trust condition, never an inherited or OR'd-together condition shared with a looser environment"). Either reading is a launch blocker or a security hole; the fix is changing `infra/prod/main.tf:3` to `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production` before applying.
- **A separate, independently-verified gap**: I grepped the entire repo for `permissions|id-token|OIDC` and the only hits are in the Terraform files (`infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf`, both `main.tf` files) — `deploy.yml` itself contains no `permissions:` block anywhere (workflow- or job-level). Requesting an OIDC token in GitHub Actions requires the job (or workflow) to declare `permissions: id-token: write`; without it, the default is no token at all. I could not find this permission granted anywhere in `deploy.yml`, for either the staging or the prod job. Combined with `scripts/deploy.sh` not existing (Answer 3) — which is presumably where the actual token-exchange/role-assumption call would live — I cannot confirm that staging authentication actually works end-to-end today either; I can only confirm the intended design shape from the Terraform module, not a verified working credential exchange.

## Next steps

If I were allowed to make changes, in this order:
1. Fix `infra/prod/main.tf:3` to `environment:production` and add `permissions: id-token: write` to the relevant job(s) in `deploy.yml` — the deploy will not authenticate without both.
2. Strip `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars` down to nothing but real production values: delete `ENABLE_DEBUG_TOOLBAR`, `AUTH_RELAXED`, `PRESET_ADMIN_ACCOUNTS`, `SHADOW_SCORING_ENABLED`, `SHADOW_SCORING_SINK` outright; get an explicit answer from the billing owner on `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN` before deciding its fate.
3. Add `frontend/.env.production` with the real prod API base URL, change the pipeline to build once per target environment (or at minimum run a separate `--mode production` build) instead of promoting one staging-mode digest to both environments, and add an automated check that scans the built bundle for staging hostnames before it's allowed to ship.
4. Add the missing `Dockerfile`, `scripts/deploy.sh`, and checkout steps in the deploy jobs — the pipeline cannot run at all right now.
5. Get an explicit, confirmed answer on how/when `backend/migrations` get applied to a brand-new prod database (manual first pass vs. automatic), rather than trusting the runbook's unqualified claim.
6. Confirm whether staging and prod share a cloud account; if so, parameterize `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:7`'s role name by environment before ever applying `infra/prod`.
7. Only after 1-6: run `terraform plan` (never `apply` without an explicit go-ahead) against `infra/prod` to see the actual diff, watching for the skill's first-apply traps (DNS/cert validation timing out before delegation is complete, an empty registry blocking the first task, placeholder secrets not caught by weak-secret boot guards) — though I found no DNS/cert/registry resources modeled in this Terraform to check directly, so this is a caution to carry into the apply, not something I can confirm from the repo as evidence today.
8. Update `docs/prod-runbook.md`, which is still staging-era (untouched since `28f07ee`) and doesn't mention Terraform apply, secret rotation, or any prod-specific step at all.

## Confidence and gaps

High confidence, directly evidenced by file contents and `git` history: the tfvars copy-paste (all 6 settings byte-identical between staging/prod), the `oidc_sub_condition` copy-paste bug, the shadow-scoring flag not actually gating the sink write (proven by reading `config.py`'s function body and its own comment), the single staging-mode frontend build being reused for both environments (proven by the workflow's own step name and the absence of any `.env.production`), and the absence of `Dockerfile`/`scripts/deploy.sh`/any checkout in the deploy jobs/any `terraform apply` step/any `permissions: id-token` block (all confirmed via `git ls-files` and full-file reads, not sampling).

Lower confidence / explicitly caveated: `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN` being a genuine dead fossil — I inferred this from naming convention and the skill's pattern, not from finding a live consumer, since this repo's backend is a trimmed stub with no billing module to check. The exact runtime consequence of the OIDC `sub` mismatch (hard auth failure vs. silent cross-environment trust) depends on enforcement semantics of whatever real cloud system the synthetic `synthetic_iam_role`/`synthetic_app_service` resources stand in for — I inferred the standard/expected exact-match behavior but couldn't verify against a real provider. Whether staging and prod share a cloud account (which determines whether the hardcoded `medislot-deploy` role name actually collides) — nothing in the repo defines account/subscription scoping either way, so this is flagged as a risk to confirm, not a proven collision. I did not attempt to verify DNS/certificate or container-registry first-apply concerns because no such resources are modeled anywhere in this Terraform — there is nothing in the repo to check for those, so I've listed them only as generic cautions for the eventual apply, not as findings. Finally, whether staging's deploy has ever actually succeeded end-to-end (given the same missing-script/missing-checkout issues would seem to apply to it too) I could not resolve from the repo alone — the committed `terraform.tfstate` for staging proves *infra* was applied at some point, but says nothing about whether the *application* deploy pipeline itself has ever completed successfully.

- Ran `find . -maxdepth 3 …` from the workspace root — discovered this workspace holds many unrelated repos, then confirmed via the root `README.md` (`README.md:3-21`) that "Each directory is its own git repository" and `medislot/` is the one described as "MediSlot, clinic scheduling SaaS (staging + prod infra)". I scoped the rest of the review to `medislot/`.
- Ran `find . -not -path './.git/*' -type f | sort` inside `medislot/` — full file inventory (17 tracked files).
- Ran `git log --oneline --all --graph`, `git branch -a`, `git status`, `git remote -v` inside `medislot/` — one branch (`main`), two commits, clean working tree, no remote configured.

- Ran `find`/`git ls-files` for `*deploy*` and `Dockerfile*` inside `medislot/` — confirmed neither `scripts/deploy.sh` nor any `Dockerfile` exists anywhere in the tracked tree.

sections.answers:
### 1. Production configuration: values that should not be there

`infra/prod/` was produced by copying `infra/staging/` (commit `2f6572d`, "REL-140 add production infra (copied from staging)", 2026-07-02) and the copy is essentially byte-for-byte in the risky places. Diffing `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars` against `infra/staging/app.auto.tfvars` line-by-line, all 6 settings are identical:

1. **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:2` — `ENABLE_DEBUG_TOOLBAR = "true"`.** A developer-convenience flag (consumed at `backend/config.py:8`). Should not be set at all in prod — per the skill's do-not-inherit rule, this class of flag should be **omitted entirely**, not set to `"false"`, since a wholesale-copied block later could silently reintroduce a bare `true`. Delete the line.
2. **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:3` — `AUTH_RELAXED = "true"`.** Relaxed-auth flag (`backend/config.py:9`). This is the single most dangerous line in the file — it ships weakened authentication to the first real production launch of a clinic-scheduling system handling patient/tenant data. Delete the line entirely.
3. **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:4` — `PRESET_ADMIN_ACCOUNTS = "qa@example.com:superadmin"`.** An allow-listed test account with superadmin privileges (`backend/config.py:10`). Exactly the "allow-list of internal/test accounts with elevated privileges" the skill flags. Delete the line; a real production admin-provisioning process should replace it, not a preset QA credential.
4. **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:5` — `SHADOW_SCORING_ENABLED = "false"`.** Looks inert because it's `"false"`, but I verified in `backend/config.py:18-22` that `record_shadow_observation()` gates purely on whether `shadow_scoring_sink` is non-empty — the comment on `config.py:19-20` states outright: "this path does not consult shadow_scoring_enabled." So this flag does not actually gate anything; it should be removed together with (5) below, not left as `"false"` under the false assumption that "false" makes it safe.
5. **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:6` — `SHADOW_SCORING_SINK = "s3://medislot-shadow/staging/observations/"`.** This is the value that actually controls the behavior in (4), and it's non-empty — so as committed, this would make prod write shadow-scoring observations (per the code path at `config.py:21-22`) to a bucket path that is itself labeled `staging`. This is precisely the skill's warning that "a stray non-empty value elsewhere in the same config can silently re-arm" a supposedly-disabled feature. Delete the line; if shadow scoring is genuinely wanted in prod later, it needs its own prod-labeled sink and a config path that actually respects the enabled flag.
6. **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:7` — `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN = "arn:synthetic:secrets:legacy-billing-key"`.** Named "legacy" in both the tfvars key and the ARN value itself, and only ever read into a config dict (`config.py:13`) with no other consumer visible in this repo. This matches the skill's "known dead fossil from a previous architecture" pattern closely enough that I'd flag it, but I could not find a live consumer of this value to prove it's dead outright (the backend here is a trimmed-down stub with no billing module). **Recommendation is lower-confidence than 1-5: confirm with whoever owns billing whether this ARN is still needed before deciding to drop or keep it** — don't carry it forward by default just because staging still references it.
7. **`infra/prod/main.tf:3` — `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"`.** Unlike the six settings above, this line should *not* be deleted — a scoped trust condition belongs in prod. But the value is wrong: it's copied verbatim from `infra/staging/main.tf:3` and still says `environment:staging`. It needs to read `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production`. Full consequence analysis is in Answer 4, since this is fundamentally a CI-authentication question.

Two things I verified were *not* copy-paste mistakes, for contrast: `infra/prod/main.tf:7` correctly names the app service `medislot-prod` (vs. staging's `medislot-staging`), and `infra/prod/README.md:1-2` correctly documents itself as "copied from infra/staging ahead of the first launch (REL-140). Not yet applied." So whoever did the copy was inconsistent — they updated the app-service name and wrote an honest README, but did not update the tfvars or the OIDC condition, and did not scrub the dev/test/shadow settings.

### 2. How the production frontend is built and deployed, and where the shipped API base URL comes from

Per `.github/workflows/deploy.yml`, there is exactly **one** frontend build, shared by both environments:

1. `build-frontend` job checks out the repo (`deploy.yml:11`), runs `npm ci` in `frontend/` (`deploy.yml:12-13`), then runs `npx vite build --mode staging` in `frontend/` (`deploy.yml:14-15`) — the `--mode staging` is a hardcoded literal, not parameterized by target environment.
2. Vite's mode-based env loading means `--mode staging` loads `frontend/.env.staging`, whose only line is `VITE_API_BASE_URL=https://staging-api.example.com` (`frontend/.env.staging:1`). There is no `.env.production` anywhere in the repo (confirmed via `git ls-files`).
3. `frontend/vite.config.ts:3` documents the mechanism directly: "VITE_-prefixed variables are inlined into the built bundle at build time." `frontend/src.txt:1-2` (a placeholder standing in for the real app source) confirms the app reads this via `import.meta.env.VITE_API_BASE_URL` — i.e., Vite performs a static, build-time substitution; the string `https://staging-api.example.com` becomes a literal in the compiled JS, not something read from the environment at runtime.
4. That bundle is packaged into a container image at repo root — `docker build -t registry.example.com/medislot:latest .` / `docker push …` (`deploy.yml:19-20`) — and the resulting digest is captured (`deploy.yml:21`) and exposed as the job output `digest` (`deploy.yml:8-9`).
5. `deploy-staging` (`deploy.yml:22-28`) deploys that digest to staging.
6. `deploy-prod` (`deploy.yml:29-35`) needs `[build-frontend, deploy-staging]` and its step is explicitly named **"deploy the same already-built digest to prod"** (`deploy.yml:34`), running `./scripts/deploy.sh "$digest" prod` — it does not rebuild anything.

Net effect: the exact same compiled bundle — with `https://staging-api.example.com` permanently inlined — is what ships to production. There is no `--mode production` build, no `.env.production` file to even supply a different value if a rebuild were added, and no step that rebuilds for prod at all. This is the skill's "rebuild per environment; don't promote a build artifact wholesale" trap, and it's not hypothetical — it's stated in the workflow's own step name. As committed, the first production users would load a frontend that calls the **staging** API for every request.

### 3. Step-by-step deploy pipeline for this first release, and what's missing

`deploy.yml` triggers on `push` to `main` (`deploy.yml:2-4`). Step by step as authored:

1. **`build-frontend`**: checkout → `npm ci` → `vite build --mode staging` → `docker build -t registry.example.com/medislot:latest .` at repo root → `docker push` → capture digest as job output.
2. **`deploy-staging`** (needs `build-frontend`, `environment: staging`): run `./scripts/deploy.sh <digest> staging`.
3. **`deploy-prod`** (needs `build-frontend` **and** `deploy-staging`, `environment: production`): run `./scripts/deploy.sh <digest> prod`.

That's the entire pipeline — nothing else runs. Checking what this first release actually requires against what's present, I found several missing pieces, each independently confirmed:

- **No Terraform apply anywhere in CI.** Neither job runs `terraform init/plan/apply` against `infra/staging` or `infra/prod`. `infra/staging/terraform.tfstate` exists and was committed in the same commit that added `deploy.yml` (`28f07ee`), which — combined with there being no apply step in the pipeline — indicates staging's state was produced by a manual/out-of-band apply, not by this CI. `infra/prod` has no `.tfstate` file at all (confirmed by directory listing), consistent with the task framing and with `infra/prod/README.md:2` ("Not yet applied."). Nothing in this repository will apply `infra/prod/` for the pipeline to deploy into — that's a manual step someone still has to do, and it isn't documented anywhere I could find.
- **`Dockerfile` does not exist.** `docker build -t … .` runs at repo root (`deploy.yml:19`, no `working-directory` override on that step), but `find -iname 'Dockerfile*'` and `git ls-files` both come back empty. As committed, the `build-frontend` job's docker build step cannot succeed.
- **`scripts/deploy.sh` does not exist.** Both `deploy-staging` and `deploy-prod` invoke it (`deploy.yml:28,35`); it is not present anywhere in `git ls-files`.
- **`deploy-staging` and `deploy-prod` never check out the repository.** Only the `build-frontend` job has an `actions/checkout` step (`deploy.yml:11`). GitHub Actions jobs don't share a filesystem across jobs except via explicit artifacts/outputs, so even if `scripts/deploy.sh` existed in the repo, it would not be present on the runner for these two jobs regardless.
- **The migrations claim in the runbook is unverified and likely false as wired.** `docs/prod-runbook.md:6` states "Database migrations are applied automatically during deploy," but `deploy.yml` contains no reference to `backend/migrations`, `alembic`, or any migration tool, and (per the point above) there's no Dockerfile/entrypoint in the repo to hide such logic in either. This is exactly the skill's "automated deploy pipelines commonly don't run your schema migrations for you on the very first setup" trap. For a brand-new prod database, someone needs to confirm explicitly how `0001_base`, `0002_tenants`, `0003_billing` (`backend/migrations/versions/*.py`, all currently stub `upgrade(): pass` bodies) actually get applied.
- **The runbook is stale relative to the prod launch it's meant to cover.** `git show --stat` shows `docs/prod-runbook.md` was only ever touched in commit `28f07ee` (the staging-only commit, 2026-04-06); the prod-infra commit `2f6572d` (2026-07-02) touched only `infra/prod/*` and never revisited the runbook. The runbook's own text says "last updated: 3 months ago" (`docs/prod-runbook.md:3`), which lines up with the staging-only commit date — further evidence it predates prod entirely and doesn't cover Terraform apply, secret rotation, or any prod-specific first-launch step.
- **The image tag is a mutable `:latest`**, not a per-commit tag (`deploy.yml:19-20`); the pipeline does capture a resolved digest for the actual deploy calls, which somewhat mitigates ambiguity, but nothing prevents a second concurrent build from clobbering `:latest` mid-run.
- **The shared `deploy_role` module hardcodes the IAM-role-equivalent name.** `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:7` sets `name = "medislot-deploy"` as a literal string, not parameterized by environment, and both `infra/staging/main.tf` and `infra/prod/main.tf` instantiate this same module. Nothing in this repo defines separate cloud accounts/subscriptions per environment, so I can't confirm whether staging and prod target the same account — but if they do, applying `infra/prod` would attempt to create a same-named role that staging already owns, which is either an apply-time failure or a takeover, on top of the trust-condition bug in Answer 4.

In short: as committed, this pipeline cannot complete a deploy to either environment (missing Dockerfile, missing deploy script, missing checkout), and even once those are fixed, nothing in it provisions `infra/prod` or proves migrations run — both are currently manual, unautomated, and largely undocumented steps standing between "merge REL-140" and "production is actually live."

### 4. How CI authenticates to staging today, and how it will authenticate to prod

The trust mechanism is modeled the same way in both environments: `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:6-11` defines a `synthetic_iam_role.deploy` whose `assume_role_condition` is `"token.actions.example.com:sub" = var.oidc_sub_condition"` — i.e., an OIDC-federated role trust condition keyed on the `sub` claim of a GitHub Actions OIDC token, matching a specific value passed in per environment.

- **Staging**: `infra/staging/main.tf:1-4` instantiates this module with `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"`. `deploy.yml:25` sets `environment: staging` on the `deploy-staging` job. GitHub mints OIDC-token `sub` claims of the form `repo:<org>/<repo>:environment:<name>` for jobs that declare an `environment:`, so the intended design is: the staging job's token carries `sub = repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging`, which matches the staging role's condition, letting it assume `medislot-deploy` in whatever account backs staging.
- **Production, as currently committed**: `deploy.yml:32` sets `environment: production` on the `deploy-prod` job, so its real OIDC token would carry `sub = repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production`. But `infra/prod/main.tf:3` still has `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"` — copied verbatim from staging and never updated (confirmed identical string in both files via `Grep`). That is a hard mismatch between the environment the job will actually run as and the condition the prod role trusts. Depending on how strictly this condition is enforced by whatever real system this "synthetic" resource models (an exact-match trust-policy condition is the standard/expected behavior for this kind of OIDC role binding), this either (a) makes every prod deploy fail authentication once `infra/prod` is applied, since the real prod job's claim won't match a condition scoped to `staging`, or (b) if match semantics were ever looser, it means the "production" deploy role actually trusts the staging GitHub environment's identity — a cross-environment trust hole the skill explicitly warns against ("a stricter environment needs its own scoped trust condition, never an inherited or OR'd-together condition shared with a looser environment"). Either reading is a launch blocker or a security hole; the fix is changing `infra/prod/main.tf:3` to `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production` before applying.
- **A separate, independently-verified gap**: I grepped the entire repo for `permissions|id-token|OIDC` and the only hits are in the Terraform files (`infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf`, both `main.tf` files) — `deploy.yml` itself contains no `permissions:` block anywhere (workflow- or job-level). Requesting an OIDC token in GitHub Actions requires the job (or workflow) to declare `permissions: id-token: write`; without it, the default is no token at all. I could not find this permission granted anywhere in `deploy.yml`, for either the staging or the prod job. Combined with `scripts/deploy.sh` not existing (Answer 3) — which is presumably where the actual token-exchange/role-assumption call would live — I cannot confirm that staging authentication actually works end-to-end today either; I can only confirm the intended design shape from the Terraform module, not a verified working credential exchange.

sections.next steps:
If I were allowed to make changes, in this order:
1. Fix `infra/prod/main.tf:3` to `environment:production` and add `permissions: id-token: write` to the relevant job(s) in `deploy.yml` — the deploy will not authenticate without both.
2. Strip `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars` down to nothing but real production values: delete `ENABLE_DEBUG_TOOLBAR`, `AUTH_RELAXED`, `PRESET_ADMIN_ACCOUNTS`, `SHADOW_SCORING_ENABLED`, `SHADOW_SCORING_SINK` outright; get an explicit answer from the billing owner on `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN` before deciding its fate.
3. Add `frontend/.env.production` with the real prod API base URL, change the pipeline to build once per target environment (or at minimum run a separate `--mode production` build) instead of promoting one staging-mode digest to both environments, and add an automated check that scans the built bundle for staging hostnames before it's allowed to ship.
4. Add the missing `Dockerfile`, `scripts/deploy.sh`, and checkout steps in the deploy jobs — the pipeline cannot run at all right now.
5. Get an explicit, confirmed answer on how/when `backend/migrations` get applied to a brand-new prod database (manual first pass vs. automatic), rather than trusting the runbook's unqualified claim.
6. Confirm whether staging and prod share a cloud account; if so, parameterize `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:7`'s role name by environment before ever applying `infra/prod`.
7. Only after 1-6: run `terraform plan` (never `apply` without an explicit go-ahead) against `infra/prod` to see the actual diff, watching for the skill's first-apply traps (DNS/cert validation timing out before delegation is complete, an empty registry blocking the first task, placeholder secrets not caught by weak-secret boot guards) — though I found no DNS/cert/registry resources modeled in this Terraform to check directly, so this is a caution to carry into the apply, not something I can confirm from the repo as evidence today.
8. Update `docs/prod-runbook.md`, which is still staging-era (untouched since `28f07ee`) and doesn't mention Terraform apply, secret rotation, or any prod-specific step at all.
sections.plan: I set out to review MediSlot's not-yet-applied production Terraform/config (`infra/prod/`) against its staging counterpart before the scheduled first-launch `terraform apply`, and to answer four specific questions: (1) which committed production config values are wrong and why, (2) how the frontend is built/deployed and where its API base URL comes from, (3) what the deploy pipeline does end-to-end for this first release and what it's missing, and (4) how CI authenticates to staging vs. how it will authenticate to prod. Per the loaded `staging-to-prod-cutover-campaign` skill, my approach was to (a) probe the actual repo/git state rather than trust the docs' claims, (b) run the "do-not-inherit scrub" checklist (dev-convenience flags, shadow/experimental flags, test-account allow-lists, dead-fossil secrets, deploy-role trust conditions) against `infra/prod/` diffed against `infra/staging/`, and (c) check whether client-facing config (the frontend API base URL) is baked into a build artifact that gets promoted wholesale rather than rebuilt per environment. This is a read-only review — I made no writes, commits, or applies.
